How to Build a Great CLI Tool

By Jane Smith — March 15, 2025

A good CLI tool does one thing well. It accepts structured input, produces structured output, and fails loudly with meaningful error messages.

The Unix philosophy has stood the test of time for a reason: composability. When each tool does one thing and communicates via stdin/stdout, you get powerful pipelines for free.

Design Principles

Start with the interface. Write the --help text before writing a single line of implementation code. This forces you to think about what the tool actually does from the user's perspective.

Exit codes matter. Zero means success. Non-zero means failure. Use specific codes for specific failure modes so callers can branch on them.

Error Handling

Print errors to stderr, never stdout. This keeps your output pipeable even when things go wrong. Use a consistent prefix like "error:" so users can grep for failures in batch output.

For recoverable warnings, use a "warning:" prefix and continue. Let users decide whether warnings are acceptable for their use case.
